events.content:
[{"role": "question", "text": "Karen Holthaus (on behalf of John Tower) [2183]: Hi, this is Karen Holthaus on for John Tower. Just digging a little bit more into technology, some of the off-premise stuff. Could you, maybe I missed some prepared comments, but just what the overall off-premise mix for the quarter was. And then virtual brands are going to play a role, but just qualitatively maybe how to think about delivery versus order ahead and pick up channels. And if any sort of macro weakness might be impacting the delivery channel.", "speaker": "Karen Holthaus (on behalf of John Tower)", "title": "Analyst, Citi"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Michael Ware [2217]: Karen, the off-premise for Chili's was around 29%, and off-premise for Maggiano's was 25%, so both hanging in there pretty steady. The breakout's still about 50-50 between delivery and to-go.", "speaker": "Michael Ware", "title": "Vice President of Finance and Investor Relations"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Karen Holthaus (on behalf of John Tower) [2235]: Sorry for maybe beating a dead horse here on some of the labor changes. At this point, it do you think you're where you, there was a mention in the prepared comments about maybe needing some more investments at front of house. So could you maybe talk about where you've kind of gotten to in terms of tables per server and where you say, if you think that's where it needs to be and is the idea that that might still grind lower, but just because of tip wages, there's really not that many dollars attached to it.", "speaker": "Karen Holthaus (on behalf of John Tower)", "title": "Analyst, Citi"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Kevin Hockman [2263]: Yeah. I think the way to think about it is there are, we are going to be making some changes, but they're going to be self-funded changes, right? So we, When we look at the simplification that we're doing, it is ginning up hours to reinvest back into the business. But I don't anticipate us needing to put additional dollars at this point in the labor model. I do think there are some changes that we need to make to the front of the house to get the improvements as strong as we've seen in the heart of the house. So if you go into the restaurants, you can feel a palpable difference in the kitchens. It's just easier to run. And I think we're seeing it in our food grade scores. Our speed scores are better. pretty much everything in the restaurant is better based on the heart of house improvements. We've seen some improvements in the front of house, but it hasn't been all the way to bright. So, for example, server attentiveness is much better, and that's leading to lower guests having a problem, which is the key metric that we look at every day in and day out, right? But when you actually talk to the restaurant teams in the front of the house, it doesn't feel as easier like it does in the heart of house. So I'm just being really candid and transparent with you guys about what we're working on, but I don't anticipate the need to actually put more dollars in the front house. It's going to be about shuffling roles and making sure people are clear on responsibilities.", "speaker": "Kevin Hockman", "title": "Chief Executive Officer and President"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Vaccaro [2347]: Hi, thanks, and good morning. I wanted to circle back on Chili's traffic performance, and I know you said it improves to the core, but Just so we're on the same page, would you be willing to provide some quantification here, either Chili's monthly traffic reflected in the down 5.8 for the quarter or the magnitude of the improvement you've seen relative to the industry in recent months?", "speaker": "Brian Vaccaro", "title": "Analyst, Raymond James"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Kevin Hockman [2371]: Well, I can share the relative versus the industry. I think that's pretty consistent with what's out there. So in January, we were a little over seven points versus the industry in In February, it was six, and then in March, it was two. And then we've seen continued improvement in April. So a pretty dramatic shift in the gap versus the industry trend on traffic. And then if you think about the pricing and mix improvements that we've made to the business, you can imagine what market share has been doing. So especially in March and April, we've just seen very, very significant improvements behind the advertising campaign.", "speaker": "Kevin Hockman", "title": "Chief Executive Officer and President"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Joe Taylor [2405]: Yeah, and Brian, those were the negative traffic gaps to the industry that... Kevin just walked through.", "speaker": "Joe Taylor", "title": "Chief Financial Officer"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Vaccaro [2412]: That's very helpful. And is that versus black box you're comparing to?", "speaker": "Brian Vaccaro", "title": "Analyst, Raymond James"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Kevin Hockman [2417]: Yeah, I'm sharing black box numbers, yeah.", "speaker": "Kevin Hockman", "title": "Chief Executive Officer and President"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Vaccaro [2420]: Okay, great. That's super helpful. On margins, Joe, you know, last quarter you provided some helpful guideposts as it relates to some of the store margin dynamics that were embedded in your second half guide. I was wondering if you could run us through a couple of the key dynamics in Q4, perhaps kind of what level of commodity inflation you expect Do you still expect around 100 bps of pressure on other op-eds and much movement either direction on labor?", "speaker": "Brian Vaccaro", "title": "Analyst, Raymond James"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Joe Taylor [2447]: Yeah, I think what I would expect to see as we kind of move through this quarter, Brian, is continued improvement relative as a percent of company sales on the food and beverage side of the equation. Our expectation is you'll probably have a commodity inflation rate down in the lower single-digit range, kind of let's say that 3% to 5%. percent range. The continued improvement there that will have a positive impact on the margin as it relates to food and beverage. And then frankly, the other two categories, my expectations is they probably are fairly stable as, you know, you may get a tweak here and there, you know, 10, 20 basis points, but I expect them to be fairly stable relative to their performance in this quarter as a percent of company sales. So there's some good guideposts for you.", "speaker": "Joe Taylor", "title": "Chief Financial Officer"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Vaccaro [2500]: Yeah, that's very helpful. Thank you for that. And then, Kevin, I wanted to just ask you on the value platforms. And several quarters ago now, maybe even a year, we were talking about those value platforms maybe constituting, I think it was 37% of sales, if you were to include three for me, emails, and perhaps some other offers. But where has that percentage declined to in recent months? And I'm curious on three for me specifically, What type of mix are you currently seeing between the 1099 and higher tiers? Thank you.", "speaker": "Brian Vaccaro", "title": "Analyst, Raymond James"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Kevin Hockman [2535]: So it's a great question. So if you recall, last quarter we talked about it being about 30% from 37% when I first started. This quarter, we're sub-30%. We're in the mid-29%. So you'd say, okay, is that continued improvement? It is given, you know, we went on air with obviously a promotional value in March with heavy TV and digital. As far as the mix within the tiers, so we were at one-third, 1099, two-thirds, the 1399 and 1599 tier. That's changed a little bit just based on the TV advertising that we did. So I think we went from 33% to about 38% at the 1099 tier, so not that significant. And then the balance, which is 62%, is in the higher 1399 and 1599 tiers. And that's a really encouraging thing because we have a very different merchandising strategy this time around with TV advertising than we had when we last advertised, which is we don't plaster all of the great values everywhere in the restaurant. You can certainly find them if you come in after seeing the ad. You can certainly find the 1099 deal. It's very clear on the menu, but we're not, you know, putting it on all of the windows and all of the, you know, the table tents and, you know, shouting it from the rafters because we want folks that were going to come in to buy a fajita or anything full price to be able to get that, but those that, you know, came in for the deal to make sure they're able to find it, right, but not just put it in front of them. So that's really yielded a much better profitable result from the advertising, and it's really exceeded our expectations not just from top line but also the bottom line.", "speaker": "Kevin Hockman", "title": "Chief Executive Officer and President"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Chris Carril [2646]: Hi. Thanks. Good morning. So, Kevin, you touched on it, I think, briefly in your prepared remarks, but could you expand maybe a bit more on what you're seeing in your bar business? I know that's been a focus area for you. but curious as to what you're seeing in terms of mix that you're getting from that part of the business, and maybe to what extent any change there has aided margins.", "speaker": "Chris Carril", "title": "Analyst, RBC Capital Markets"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Kevin Hockman [2668]: Yeah, so certainly the attention to the bar, we're seeing incremental mix, both PPA, so check, as well as the percentage of sales coming from the bar. What's also helping is dine-in traffic was up pretty significantly this quarter versus a year ago, so That obviously helps with alcohol mix because any dine-in traffic is going to have way more attachment to alcohol than to-go or virtual brand, right? Or virtual brand doesn't have any alcohol mix. So that's all headed in the right direction. I will say with the main menu update, we have pretty significant alcohol innovation coming, a whole new margarita lineup. The front page of the menu is going to be about Premium margaritas, as well as we'll still have the margarita of the month that you can get for $6 or $7, depending on the margarita. We're bringing innovation also into the promotional strategy. So if you were in our Chili's last month, we had a Patron margarita of the month for $7. For those of you familiar with tequila, that's a very premium tequila. We're also going to be bringing in the Casamigos brand as main going forward, which is super exciting because we know some guests, you know, they're willing to pay up more for, you know, something that's super premium. So, and you're going to see things like a skinny margarita hit the menu where, you know, some customers want a hundred calorie margarita and it's being made with, you know, a very premium, the rocks tequila, which is Termano Blanco. So we've got a lot of exciting things coming to the bar. And so we're seeing some good progress with alcohol mix and PPA, but I think, you know, bringing some excitement and innovation into the bar, I think is going to help accelerate that.", "speaker": "Kevin Hockman", "title": "Chief Executive Officer and President"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Chris Carril [2768]: Great. Thanks. And then, Joe, you touched on, I think, 4Q labor margin a couple of minutes ago. But could you expand maybe a bit more on kind of what you're thinking from a wage inflation outlook perspective, maybe in the 4Q and just kind of trend beyond there?", "speaker": "Chris Carril", "title": "Analyst, RBC Capital Markets"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Joe Taylor [2787]: Yeah, I think it's going to remain pretty steady in that 5% range, you know, give or take a little, Chris. And, you know, going forward, I'll give you that kind of thought process as we head into our 24 guidance. But you're seeing, you know, on a macro level, what I see is continued slight improvement, improvement being lower wage rate inflation. I think that is going to work its way into the restaurant systems as we kind of go forward. But staying right now, kind of continuing in that 5% range.", "speaker": "Joe Taylor", "title": "Chief Financial Officer"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Fred Whiteman [2834]: Hey, guys. Good morning. Thanks for the question. I just want to follow up maybe on the Chili's dine-in comment. I think you said that that was positive throughout the quarter. I'm wondering if that followed sort of a similar pattern or similar cadence to the market share stats that you guys shared. Was there a pretty big inflection in March as well?", "speaker": "Fred Whiteman", "title": "Analyst, Wolf Research"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Joe Taylor [2854]: It was pretty consistent across the quarter. So we actually started to see that improvement of more guests coming back in to the dining rooms at the beginning of the quarter and remained with a small uptick as we kind of headed through the March. You would expect, obviously, the campaign to impact across all of our channels, and it did. But it wasn't driven solely by the campaign. It predated the beginning of the campaign. So that's good to see. That's where we want guests coming.", "speaker": "Joe Taylor", "title": "Chief Financial Officer"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Fred Whiteman [2886]: Makes sense. And then I think last quarter you had said that you were expecting to exit the year at around 8% on price. Is that still a reasonable estimate, or has that changed?", "speaker": "Fred Whiteman", "title": "Analyst, Wolf Research"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Joe Taylor [2895]: I think we'll be probably a little bit closer to 9% as we kind of exit looking at the data right now. Okay. Perfect. Thank you.", "speaker": "Joe Taylor", "title": "Chief Financial Officer"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Michael Ware [2906]: All right, and that concludes our call for today. We appreciate everyone joining us and look forward to seeing many of you at our Investor Day on June 7th.", "speaker": "Michael Ware", "title": "Vice President of Finance and Investor Relations"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Joe Taylor [2914]: Great. Thanks, everybody.", "speaker": "Joe Taylor", "title": "Chief Financial Officer"}]
